Notice Of Motion And Motion For Bifurcation Of Liability And Damages
Off Calendar Jul-07-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

RE: Defendant Transbay Joint Powers Authority's Motion for Bifurcation of the Affirmative Defense of Design Immunity.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling. Defendant Transbay Joint Powers Authority's Motion for Bifurcation of the Affirmative Defense of Design Immunity is DENIED WITHOUT PREJUDICE. Defendant failed to provide sufficient notice of its motion. Moving and supporting papers shall be served and filed at least 16 court days before the hearing. (Code Civ. Proc., 1005, subd. (b).) When the papers are e-served, the period of notice is extended by two court days. (Code Civ. Proc., 1010.6, subd. (a)(4)(B).) Here, Defendant e-served its moving papers, but did so only 16 court days before the hearing. Even on the merits, the court would deny the motion without prejudice to defendant renewing it before the trial judge. To be clear, after notice and hearing, the court may order that an issue or any part of a case be tried before other issues or parts of the case are tried, as long as it does so "no later than the close of the pretrial conference in cases in which such pretrial conference is to be held, or, in other cases, no later than 30 days before the trial date." (See Code Civ. Proc., 598.) In this case, a pretrial conference will be held following assignment of the case to a department for trial. Any renewed motion should be filed consistent with the rules for motions in limine. (See L.R.S.F. 6.2.)

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not reported. (302/JMQ)

Notice Of Defendant Transbay Joint Powers Authority?S Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiff?S Third Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Oct-19-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. MIKALAI HROSHAU, Esq. of Law Office of Mikalai Hroshau (Tel: 415-684-8660) for Plaintiff Minying Yang;
Atty. CHRISTINA M. FORST, Esq. of Allen Glaessner Hazelwood Werth (Tel: 415-697-2000) for Defendant Transbay Joint Powers Authority.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT TRANSBAY JOINT POWERS AUTHORITY's Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiff's Third Amended Complaint.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling:

Defendant Transbay Joint Powers Authority's motion to strike portions of Plaintiff's Third Amended Complaint is denied. Plaintiff's allegation that TJPA violated the Building Code in constructing the glass wall may be admissible under a negligence per se theory under her second cause of action for negligence, to which Defendant has not demurred, or under her fourth cause of action, to show that a negligent act of an employee created the dangerous condition of public property. (See Evid. Code Section 669; Huang v. Garner (1984) 157 Cal.App.3d 404, 412 [holding that "the evidence regarding violations of the Uniform Building Code was sufficient to allow the case to go to the jury upon an instruction as to negligence per se"].)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)

DEMURRER to 3RD Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-19-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. MIKALAI HROSHAU, Esq. of Law Office of Mikalai Hroshau (Tel: 415-684-8660) for Plaintiff Minying Yang;
Atty. CHRISTINA M. FORST, Esq. of Allen Glaessner Hazelwood Werth (Tel: 415-697-2000) for Defendant Transbay Joint Powers Authority.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT TRANSBAY JOINT POWERS AUTHORITY'S DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling:

SUSTAINED without leave to amend as to the first cause of action for negligence/vicarious liability and overruled as to the fourth cause of action for premises liability.

Plaintiff alleges that on September 24, 2018, she ran into a clear unmarked glass wall at the Salesforce Transit Center, breaking two of her front teeth. (TAC 9.) In her first cause of action for negligence/vicarious liability, she alleges that the glass wall was constructed by "either TJPA employees or independent contractors employed by TJPA who failed to properly mark the glass wall and/or violated California Building Code Section 2406" in building the wall. (Id. 17.) As the Court explained in sustaining Defendant's demurrer to plaintiff's first amended complaint, public entities like the TJPA may be liable only if a statute so declares. (Gov. Code Sec. 815, 815.2; Cochran v. Herzog Engraving Co. (1984) 155 Cal.App.3d 405, 409 ["under the statutory scheme in California, all government tort liability must be based on statute. . . . [P]ublic entities may be liable only if a statute declares them to be liable."].) Government Code section 835 "sets out the exclusive conditions under which a public entity is liable for injuries caused by a dangerous condition of public property." (Brown v. Poway Unified School Dist. (1993) 4 Cal.4th 820, 829; Nishihama v. City and County of San Francisco (2001) 93 Cal.App.4th 298, 302 ["it would have been error to instruct the jury that the City would be liable, on a theory of respondeat superior, for the ordinary negligence of its employees"].) In insisting that TJPA can be held vicariously liable for the negligence of its employees on the basis of respondent superior, Plaintiff ignores this controlling authority. Plaintiff does not show a reasonable probability that she can amend to avoid this fundamental defect. Accordingly, the demurrer to the first cause of action for negligence/vicarious liability is sustained without leave to amend.

In the fourth cause of action, Plaintiff attempts to state a cause of action for "premises liability" under Government Code section 835. Defendant objects to Plaintiff's allegation in that cause of action to "the regulations set up in California Building Code." (TAC 28.) However, "a party may not demur to a portion of a cause of action." (PH II, Inc. v. Superior Court (1995) 33 Cal.App.4th 1680, 1681.) As Defendant concedes, the fourth cause of action states a claim for dangerous condition of public property under Government Code section 835. Accordingly, Defendant's demurrer to the fourth cause of action is overruled.

Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the court's ruling.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-02-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances.

RULING: There being no appearance, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT TRANSBAY JOINT POWERS AUTHORITY's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT, as follows:

Defendant Transbay Joint Powers Authority's (TJPA) demurrers to the first amended complaint's third, fourth and fifth causes of action are sustained with leave to amend. Public entities like the TJPA may be liable only if a statute so declares. (Cochran v. Herzog (1984) 155 Cal.App.3d 405, 409; Govt. Code 815, 815.2.) Plaintiff relies on "Building Code Section 2406," which is a section heading in regulations. Section 2406 is not a statute and makes no declaration as to public entities. Further, "negligence per se" is not a cause of action (Quiroz v. Seventh Ave. Center (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 1256, 1285), and no authority is cited for a "special relationship" between a transit passenger and the TJPA.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com
